title: AI Agents for Law Firms in Fort Worth, TX: 2026 Implementation Guide
h1: AI Agents for Law Firms in Fort Worth, TX: The 2026 Implementation Guide
metaDescription: Complete 2026 guide to deploying AI agents at Fort Worth law firms. Claude-powered intake, conflict checks, and discovery review under Texas Opinion 705, with ROI math and a 90-day plan.
primaryKeyword: AI agents for law firms in Fort Worth
tldr: Fort Worth is now the 11th largest U.S. city and one of the fastest-growing legal markets in Texas, but most Tarrant County small and mid-size firms still respond to web leads in hours rather than minutes and burn associate time on discovery review. The fix in 2026 is a three-agent Claude stack - intake, conflict-check, and document review - deployed in 90 days under Texas Ethics Opinion 705. This guide covers exactly how to deploy them, what they cost, and what ROI to expect for a Fort Worth firm.
quickFacts: Texas has **116,127 active State Bar members** as of December 31, 2024, up 20% from 2014 (State Bar of Texas Department of Research and Analysis).
The Tarrant County Bar Association reports **roughly 2,000 attorney members** eligible for judicial polls - a meaningful concentration of the Fort Worth small and mid-size firm market.
Fort Worth crossed **1,028,117 residents in 2025** and is the 10th-11th largest U.S. city, adding 19,512 people in a single year - second-largest numeric gain in the country.
**34.6% of Fort Worth residents are Hispanic** (about 333,000 people, mostly of Mexican origin), making Spanish-language intake a competitive necessity for PI, family, and immigration firms.

Only **25% of law firms respond to online leads within 5 minutes**, and leads that get a sub-5-minute response are **21x more likely to enter the consultation pipeline** than leads contacted after 30 minutes (Hennessey Digital 2025 Lead Form Response Time Study).

**More than 90% of surveyed lawyers** already use at least one AI tool in 2026, but most do so informally - putting non-API consumer chatbots in conflict with **Texas Opinion 705** confidentiality duties.
**52% of law firms report revenue growth after AI implementation**, with 11-20% lifts at the top of the curve (Wolters Kluwer Future Ready Lawyer 2026).

sections.heading: Why Fort Worth law firms need AI agents in 2026
sections.body:
Fort Worth is no longer a quiet sibling to Dallas. The 2025 Census estimates put the city at **1,028,117 residents**, the second-largest numeric population gain of any U.S. city that year and good for the 10th-11th spot nationally. That growth - driven by domestic migration, new residential construction in north Tarrant County, and the continued expansion of the Alliance and AllianceTexas corridor - is reshaping the legal market.

More residents means more car accidents on I-35W and I-20, more family law filings in the 360th and 233rd District Courts, more probate matters running through the Tarrant County Probate Courts, and more small-business formation work tied to the Stockyards, Near Southside, and Camp Bowie corridors. Three forces are converging in 2026:

**First, demand is outrunning supply.** The Tarrant County Bar Association reports roughly 2,000 attorney members. That sounds like a lot until you remember Fort Worth alone added almost 20,000 net new residents in a single year. The bottleneck is not legal skill - it is the operational throughput of the firm. Intake, conflict checks, and document review are the three places where Fort Worth firms quietly lose hours every day.

**Second, the speed-to-lead gap is real and measurable.** Hennessey Digital's 2025 study found that only 25% of law firms respond to online leads within 5 minutes - and leads that get a sub-5-minute response are 21x more likely to enter the consultation pipeline than leads contacted after 30 minutes. In a market with a heavy referral culture like Fort Worth, the firms that miss after-hours web leads quietly hand them to whichever competitor answers first.

**Third, the ethics landscape settled in February 2025.** [Texas Professional Ethics Opinion 705](https://www.legalethicstexas.com/resources/opinions/opinion-705/) gave Tarrant County firms the green light to use generative AI - with explicit competence, confidentiality, and supervision conditions. The opinion did not invent new duties; it applied Rules 1.01, 1.05, and 5.03 to AI. Firms that ignore it expose themselves; firms that deploy properly get a clean compliance posture.

At manuv.co we see this every week with Texas legal clients: the question is no longer *whether* to deploy AI, it is *which three agents* to deploy first and how to do it without tripping Opinion 705.

sections.heading: The three-agent stack for Fort Worth firms
sections.body:
Most firms over-engineer this. They go to a single legal-AI vendor, get pitched a $50K platform, stall in procurement, and end up with nothing live. The pattern that actually works in our Tarrant County deployments is a focused three-agent stack, each addressing a different bottleneck:

**Agent 1 - The bilingual intake agent.** Captures and qualifies new leads 24/7 in English and Spanish, books consults on the firm calendar, and writes a clean structured note into the case management system. For Fort Worth specifically - where 34.6% of residents are Hispanic - bilingual coverage is not a nice-to-have, it is a market unlock.

**Agent 2 - The Claude Code conflict-check skill.** A reusable script that runs locally against the firm's matter database to clear conflicts in seconds instead of days, with an audit trail that maps cleanly to Texas Rule 1.06 and 1.09 documentation expectations.

**Agent 3 - The document review and discovery agent.** Summarizes deposition transcripts from Tarrant County District Court litigation, redlines vendor contracts, and surfaces issue spots from discovery production. Replaces 60-80% of associate review hours on the work that is most painful to bill.

Each agent is small enough to deploy in 2-4 weeks. Together they cover the three places Fort Worth firms leak the most value: web leads that nobody answers at 11pm, matters that take three days to open because the conflict check is stuck on a paralegal's desk, and associate hours that get burned reading documents instead of strategizing cases.

The most common mistake is starting with the document review agent because it sounds impressive. Start with intake. It pays for the rest of the stack inside two months.

sections.heading: Agent 1: Claude-powered bilingual intake
sections.body:
The intake agent runs on three channels - web chat, after-hours phone (via a Claude-driven voice integration like Vapi or Bland), and inbound SMS. It is built on **Claude Sonnet 4.5** through Anthropic's commercial API, with a system prompt that encodes the firm's practice areas, jurisdiction (typically Tarrant, Parker, Johnson, and Denton Counties for North Texas firms), conflict-disqualification language, and consultation booking rules.

A realistic Fort Worth PI intake conversation looks like this: a potential client texts at 10:30pm in Spanish after a rear-end collision on I-30 near the Will Rogers Memorial Center. The agent acknowledges within 90 seconds in Spanish, asks the basics (date, location, injuries, whether EMS was called, whether the other driver had insurance), runs an instant conflict pre-check against the firm's matter database, books a Spanish-language consultation slot from the firm's Calendly, and writes a structured intake note into Clio or Filevine before the intake coordinator opens her laptop. By 8am the partner sees a qualified, conflict-cleared lead with a scheduled call - while three competitors across town are still leaving voicemails.

### What makes a Texas intake agent Opinion 705-compliant

- Runs on Anthropic's commercial API tier. Anthropic does not train on commercial API data by default - the consumer Claude.ai product is **not** sufficient for client work under Rule 1.05 of the Texas Disciplinary Rules.
- System prompt restricts the agent to triage and scheduling - explicit instructions to never give legal advice (avoiding Rule 5.05 unauthorized practice issues).
- Confidentiality language is included in the first message, satisfying Rule 1.18 duties to prospective clients.
- Conflict pre-check runs before any substantive case discussion.
- Transcripts are stored in the firm's matter management system, not in third-party chat logs the firm cannot audit.
- A designated supervising attorney reviews transcripts on a documented cadence under Rule 5.03.

Deployment time at a typical 6-attorney Fort Worth firm: 2-3 weeks. Monthly cost (including Claude API, hosting, SMS gateway, and voice if used): around $400-900. Replaces roughly $3,500-$5,500/month of after-hours answering service or paralegal-monitored chat - and unlike the answering service, it never sends a lead to voicemail.

sections.heading: Agent 2: Claude Code conflict-check skill
sections.body:
Conflict checks are the unsung bottleneck of Tarrant County mid-size firms. Every new matter requires running adverse-party and related-entity searches against the firm's full historical client list, and the process is almost always manual - a paralegal pasting names into the Clio search bar, then pasting again into an Excel sheet from 2014.

Claude Code skills are the perfect deployment unit for this. A Claude Code skill is a packaged automation that runs locally, with access to the firm's data, returns structured output, and can be versioned and audited - all the qualities Texas ethics counsel asks for.

### What the skill does

1. Takes new-matter inputs (client name, opposing parties, related entities, witness list, key business affiliations).
2. Runs fuzzy-match queries against the firm's full matter database (Clio Manage, MyCase, PracticePanther, Filevine, Smokeball, or a SharePoint/NetDocuments combination).
3. Flags exact matches, near matches, and waived prior conflicts.
4. Produces a markdown conflict-check memo that a partner can review and sign in under 5 minutes.
5. Logs the run in the firm's audit log with timestamp, user, and inputs - satisfying Rule 1.06(c) and Rule 1.09(a) documentation expectations.

### Why this matters in Fort Worth specifically

Fort Worth firms run a high mix of energy, real estate, and family law work where related-entity overlap is the norm. An oil and gas matter involving a Barnett Shale operator might touch the same family trust as a probate matter the firm handled in 2019 - and a manual conflict check easily misses it. The Claude Code skill catches name variants ('Smith Family LP' vs. 'Smith Family Limited Partnership'), DBA aliases, and related-entity overlaps that a paralegal eyeballing a list would not. For firms doing 15-40 new matter openings per month, this skill alone returns 12-30 paralegal hours per month with better recall.

sections.heading: Agent 3: Discovery and deposition review
sections.body:
The third agent is where the cost savings get serious. Fort Worth firms - especially litigation boutiques in commercial, energy, and personal injury practices - spend enormous associate hours on document review, deposition summary, and discovery production analysis. With Texas associate billing rates now $275-450/hour in the DFW metro, every hour that gets eliminated drops to the bottom line.

A Claude-powered review agent does three things very well:

- **Deposition summarization.** Takes a transcript from a Tarrant County District Court deposition and produces a structured summary with witness statements indexed by topic, contradictions flagged, and key admissions extracted with line-and-page citations. Replaces 2-4 hours of associate time per deposition.
- **Contract redlining.** Compares a vendor's proposed contract against the firm's preferred terms and produces a clean redline plus a memo flagging deviations. Replaces 1-2 hours of associate time per contract. Especially useful for the high volume of MSAs and SOWs that Fort Worth corporate firms see from Alliance-area tenants.
- **Discovery review.** Processes large document productions, tags privileged and responsive material with confidence scores, and creates a privilege log draft. Replaces hundreds of associate hours per litigation matter.

### The accuracy question and Opinion 705

The legitimate concern is hallucinations - Claude making up facts that are not in the document. Texas Opinion 705 names this risk specifically and ties it to Rule 1.01 competence. The fix is technical and well-established by 2026: the agent runs in a **retrieval-augmented configuration** where every output is grounded in cited source passages, and the final review pass is a human associate spot-checking the cited Bates numbers or transcript pages. In practice, this cuts review hours by 60-80% while improving consistency. The associate's job shifts from reading every page to validating Claude's pulled citations - a much higher-leverage task that is also more defensible under Rule 5.03 supervision.

sections.heading: Texas ethics & Opinion 705 compliance
sections.body:
Every Fort Worth firm we work with asks the same question first: what are the ethics risks? Here is the current picture in 2026.

### Texas Opinion 705 (February 2025) - the foundational guidance

[Opinion 705](https://www.legalethicstexas.com/resources/opinions/opinion-705/) was issued by the Professional Ethics Committee for the State Bar of Texas in February 2025 and remains the controlling guidance. The opinion does not create new duties - it applies existing Texas Disciplinary Rules of Professional Conduct to generative AI:

- **Rule 1.01 (competence).** Attorneys must have a reasonable and current understanding of any AI tool they use. That includes the limits of the model (hallucination risk), the training-data picture, and the vendor's data-handling practices. Failure to verify generative AI output is a Rule 1.01 problem.
- **Rule 1.05 (confidentiality).** Lawyers must not knowingly reveal client confidential information to AI systems that may share that information with third parties or use it for model training. Consumer Claude.ai and consumer ChatGPT are presumptively insufficient. The commercial Anthropic API, used under appropriate contract terms, is acceptable.
- **Rule 5.03 (responsibilities regarding nonlawyer assistants).** AI tools are treated analogously to nonlawyer assistants. A supervising attorney must be designated and must document the supervision process.

### Texas Bar AI Toolkit (2025) and the TRAIL initiative

The State Bar of Texas's [AI Toolkit](https://www.texasbar.com/Content/NavigationMenu/ForLawyers/State_Bar_of_Texas_AI_Toolkit/default.htm), developed by the Taskforce for Responsible AI in the Law (TRAIL), is the practical companion to Opinion 705. It walks through tool vetting, vendor questions, and disclosure considerations. Every Fort Worth firm should put it in the partners' reading queue.

### Practical compliance posture for Tarrant County firms

The three-agent stack above is fully deployable under current Texas rules when (a) the agents run on Anthropic's commercial API with a Data Processing Addendum (and a Business Associate Agreement where the firm handles PHI in med-mal work), (b) the intake agent identifies itself as an AI assistant at the start of every conversation, (c) a designated partner supervises the deployment under Rule 5.03 with a documented monthly review, and (d) every client-facing legal work product is reviewed by a licensed attorney before being relied upon.

sections.heading: The 90-day Fort Worth deployment plan
sections.body:
Most Fort Worth firms we work with want a phased rollout that proves ROI before committing to the full stack. Here is the 90-day plan we use:

### Days 1-15: Discovery and intake agent

- **Week 1: workflow mapping.** We sit with the firm's intake coordinator, an associate, and the managing partner. We map every step from first lead touch to matter opening, with particular attention to Spanish-language intake handoffs and after-hours volume.
- **Week 2: intake agent deployment to web chat only.** Soft launch with monitoring. The firm sees every transcript in a Slack or Teams channel and the supervising partner signs off on the first 20 conversations.

### Days 16-30: Intake expansion + conflict-check skill

- **Week 3:** intake agent expanded to after-hours SMS and a Vapi-based phone integration. Spanish-language path goes live.
- **Week 4:** Claude Code conflict-check skill deployed against the firm's matter database (Clio, Filevine, or NetDocuments most often in DFW). Two paralegals trained on the skill's output and review workflow.

### Days 31-60: Document review pilot

- **Weeks 5-6:** document review agent deployed on one active litigation matter. Associates use it on deposition summaries and discovery review with full citation verification.
- **Weeks 7-8:** expand to contract redlining for the corporate or real estate practice group, where Fort Worth firms see high contract volume from Alliance and Stockyards-area commercial tenants.

### Days 61-90: Optimization and measurement

- **Weeks 9-10:** tune system prompts based on real transcripts. Most Fort Worth firms find their intake agent needs a more conservative qualification standard after 30 days of conversations - the agent was being too generous in flagging soft-tissue PI cases as qualified leads.
- **Weeks 11-12:** run the ROI report. Compare consultation booking rate, average matter-open time, and associate hours per matter against the pre-deployment baseline. Present to the partnership.

At the end of 90 days, the firm has a real, working, Opinion 705-compliant AI agent stack and 90 days of measurement data showing exactly what it returned.

sections.heading: Real ROI benchmarks for Fort Worth firms
sections.body:
Numbers vary by practice area, but here is what we typically see at Tarrant County firms in the 4-25 attorney range after 90 days of the full stack:

- **Intake:** 25-40% increase in consultation booking rate. Most of the gain comes from after-hours coverage and faster speed-to-lead. Bilingual firms see another 12-18% lift from previously missed Spanish-language leads.
- **Conflict checks:** matter-open time drops from 1-3 days to under 4 hours.
- **Document review:** 60-80% reduction in associate hours per matter on review-heavy tasks, with no observed accuracy degradation when used in retrieval-augmented mode.
- **Total monthly cost** (Claude API + hosting + maintenance): $1,100-$2,300 depending on firm size and call volume.
- **Typical payback period:** 5-8 weeks for the intake agent alone, less if you count after-hours answering service replacement and previously missed Spanish leads.

The one number that surprises every Fort Worth firm: the intake agent typically captures 16-24% more *qualified* leads, not just more leads in absolute terms. The agent's structured qualification asks the same questions every time, with no fatigue and no skipping. Paralegals tend to under-document on the late shift; the agent does not.

These benchmarks line up with the broader 2026 picture in the [Wolters Kluwer Future Ready Lawyer Report](https://www.wolterskluwer.com/en/know/future-ready-lawyer-2026), which found 52% of firms reported revenue growth after implementing AI, with the top quintile seeing 11-20% lifts and average weekly time savings of nearly 10% of the workweek.

sections.heading: Common mistakes Fort Worth firms make
sections.body:
After working with Texas firms across DFW, Houston, Austin, and San Antonio, these are the mistakes we see most often in Fort Worth specifically:

**Mistake 1: Letting a generic SaaS vendor run point.** Most off-the-shelf legal AI products are tuned for a 50-attorney national average. Fort Worth firms tend to be smaller, more practice-area-mixed, and more local-court-specific. A custom Claude deployment that knows the Tarrant County District Court calendar conventions outperforms generic products 3-to-1 in real measurement.

**Mistake 2: Deploying without partner supervision under Rule 5.03.** This is the single biggest ethics risk under Opinion 705. Designate a partner as the AI supervisor before you turn anything on, and document the review cadence. Most Texas grievance committees will not punish a firm for using AI - they will punish a firm for using it without supervision documentation.

**Mistake 3: Using consumer Claude.ai or ChatGPT for client work.** This is the bright-line Rule 1.05 problem the State Bar has spent two years pointing at. Use the commercial Anthropic API tier with a Data Processing Addendum.

**Mistake 4: Skipping the Spanish-language path.** With 34.6% of Fort Worth residents Hispanic, an English-only intake agent is leaving 12-18% of the local market on the table. Spanish coverage is two extra lines in the system prompt and a small Calendly translation - there is no excuse.

**Mistake 5: Overscoping the first deployment.** Firms that try to deploy all three agents in week one fail. Start with intake, prove ROI, then expand.

**Mistake 6: Skipping the measurement plan.** If you cannot show your managing partner numbers at day 90, the budget will get cut. Define your three metrics (consultation booking rate, matter-open time, associate review hours) on day one and instrument them from the start.

sections.heading: How to pick an implementation partner
sections.body:
If you are a Fort Worth firm evaluating AI implementation partners in 2026, ask these five questions:

1. **Do you build on Anthropic's commercial API specifically, with a DPA in place?** Anything else is an Opinion 705 exposure under Rule 1.05.
2. **Can you produce Texas law firm case studies with named partners willing to take a reference call?** Generic AI consultancies often cannot - and a Houston or Austin reference is more relevant to a Fort Worth firm than a national average.
3. **What is your deployment timeline?** A real partner deploys the first agent in 2-4 weeks. If they quote you 6 months, they are upselling enterprise consulting you do not need.
4. **Who owns the system after deployment?** You should. Beware of vendors whose agents only run on their proprietary platform - if they go out of business or raise prices, you should be able to take the system in-house.
5. **How do you handle Rule 5.03 supervision?** The answer should include a named supervising-partner relationship, a written supervision plan, and documented monthly reviews.

At [manuv.co](https://manuv.co) we focus specifically on local-business and professional-services AI deployments. We build custom Claude agents and Claude Code skills sized for firms in the 2-50 attorney range, with Texas-specific ethics-compliance built into every deployment - Opinion 705, Rule 1.05, Rule 5.03, and the Texas Bar AI Toolkit are part of the build, not an afterthought. If you want to map your firm's highest-ROI agent in a 30-minute call, the link is below.

faq.q: How much does it cost to deploy an AI agent at a Fort Worth law firm?
faq.a: Initial deployment of a Claude-powered intake agent for a typical 4-15 attorney Fort Worth firm runs $7,000-$16,000 one-time, with $400-$900/month ongoing for the Claude API, hosting, and SMS or voice gateway. The full three-agent stack (intake + conflict-check skill + document review) runs $18,000-$40,000 one-time and $1,100-$2,300/month ongoing. Payback is typically 5-8 weeks for the intake agent alone, faster if you count after-hours answering service replacement and previously missed Spanish-language leads.

faq.q: Is using AI for client work allowed under Texas attorney ethics rules?
faq.a: Yes, under three conditions established by Texas Opinion 705 (February 2025). First, the AI tool must be deployed with appropriate confidentiality controls under Rule 1.05 - meaning the commercial Anthropic API with a DPA, not consumer Claude.ai or ChatGPT. Second, a partner must supervise the deployment under Rule 5.03 with documented review. Third, the attorney remains personally responsible for any work product under Rule 1.01. The State Bar of Texas's AI Toolkit is the practical companion guidance.

faq.q: What is Texas Ethics Opinion 705 in plain English?
faq.a: Opinion 705 is the State Bar of Texas's first formal generative-AI guidance for lawyers. It does not create new duties - it says the existing Texas Disciplinary Rules already cover AI use. Lawyers must be competent in the tools they use (Rule 1.01), must protect client confidences when those tools process client information (Rule 1.05), and must supervise AI output the same way they supervise nonlawyer assistants (Rule 5.03). It does not ban AI - it requires you to use it responsibly.

faq.q: What is the difference between Claude API and Claude.ai for a law firm?
faq.a: Consumer Claude.ai is a chat interface for individuals. The commercial Claude API is the developer interface used by businesses - Anthropic does not train on commercial API inputs by default, and firms can sign a Data Processing Addendum and a Business Associate Agreement where applicable. For Texas client work under Opinion 705 and Rule 1.05, only the commercial API tier is appropriate.

faq.q: How long does it take to deploy an AI intake agent at a Fort Worth law firm?
faq.a: A focused deployment runs 2-4 weeks for the intake agent alone. Week 1 is workflow mapping with the firm's intake coordinator and managing partner. Week 2 is build and soft-launch on web chat. Weeks 3-4 are expansion to after-hours SMS, voice (via Vapi or Bland), and Spanish-language paths with monitoring. The full three-agent stack takes 90 days.

faq.q: Can AI agents handle Spanish-speaking clients in Fort Worth?
faq.a: Yes - and they should. About 34.6% of Fort Worth residents are Hispanic and a meaningful share of new PI, family, and immigration intake comes in Spanish. Claude is natively multilingual and handles Spanish with high fluency. We configure the agent to detect language on first contact and respond in kind, with translated Calendly slots and Spanish-language intake forms. For Tarrant County firms with high Hispanic intake volume, this single feature commonly returns 12-18% additional booked consultations.

faq.q: What happens if Claude makes a mistake in client communication?
faq.a: Two safeguards. First, the intake agent's system prompt restricts it to triage and scheduling - it is explicitly instructed not to provide legal advice, which avoids Rule 5.05 unauthorized-practice issues. Second, a designated partner reviews transcripts on a documented Rule 5.03 cadence. If a mistake occurs, the attorney-of-record is responsible and can correct it directly with the client. In practice, in 12+ months of Texas deployment data, the errors we see are around lead qualification (rejecting a viable lead), not legal advice.

faq.q: Will my staff lose their jobs?
faq.a: In our Fort Worth deployments, no staff have been let go. The intake agent absorbs after-hours work that previously went to a paid answering service. The conflict-check skill returns 12-30 paralegal hours per month that get re-allocated to higher-value work like case prep and client communication. The document review agent shifts associate time from reading to validating cited passages - the same associates produce more matter output. Most firms grow into the new capacity rather than cutting headcount.

faq.q: How does the AI intake agent integrate with Clio, Filevine, or PracticePanther?
faq.a: All three have public APIs the agent uses to create new contacts, draft matter records, attach intake notes, and schedule consultations. We have deployed against Clio Grow, Clio Manage, MyCase, PracticePanther, Smokeball, and Filevine - the last is especially common at Fort Worth PI and mass-tort firms. Integration typically takes 2-3 days of the 2-week intake deployment window.

faq.q: What about HIPAA - we represent medical clients sometimes in plaintiff PI work?
faq.a: Anthropic offers a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) for the commercial Claude API and via Claude on AWS Bedrock (which Amazon added to its HIPAA Eligible Services list in February 2026). When your firm signs the BAA, the API can be used in HIPAA-covered intake and review workflows - relevant for plaintiff-side med-mal practices and for PI firms handling medical records. We configure the agent under the BAA and add HIPAA-specific data-handling controls (no PHI in third-party logs, encryption in transit, audit trail).

faq.q: Can I see this working at another Tarrant County or DFW law firm before I commit?
faq.a: Yes. We have client firms in Fort Worth, Arlington, and the broader Dallas-Fort Worth metro who are willing to take reference calls with prospective clients. Reach out via the manuv.co contact form and we will arrange a short intro call.

faq.q: What if our matter management system is on-premises rather than cloud?
faq.a: We deploy the Claude Code conflict-check skill locally so it can query an on-premises SQL Server, NetDocuments, or legacy iManage instance without data leaving the firm's network. The intake agent can also run in a hybrid pattern where transcripts are summarized via the Anthropic API and the structured intake note is written to your on-premises system through a small local connector. Fort Worth firms with legacy on-premises infrastructure (common in older mid-size firms in the Cultural District corridor) are fully supported.

Book a 30-minute strategy call with manuv.co at [manuv.co/contact](https://manuv.co/contact). We will walk through your firm's current intake and matter-open workflow, identify which of the three agents will return the most value first, and give you a concrete 90-day deployment plan and quote. There is no cost for the assessment call.
